question: what are fermented vegetables called
title: What Are Fermented Vegetables Called? The Names, the Process, and Why It Matters
metaDescription: Lacto-fermented vegetables go by many names — kimchi, sauerkraut, curtido, tsukemono. Here's what they all have in common, how the process works, and why these living foods are so different from pickles.
content:
# What Are Fermented Vegetables Called? The Names, the Process, and Why It Matters

When people first hear me talk about fermented vegetables at the market, sometimes they've never put words to what they're experiencing. They know about pickles. They might know about sauerkraut. But they don't realize there's a whole family of foods built on the same biological principle — vegetables transformed by an ancient process that humans figured out long before refrigeration, long before food science, long before anyone knew what a bacterium was.

So let me name them, explain what connects them, and tell you why a fermented vegetable is not just a trend — it's one of the oldest food technologies in human history.

## The Family Name: Lacto-Fermented Vegetables

The technical name is lacto-fermented vegetables, or sometimes cultured vegetables. "Lacto" doesn't mean dairy — it refers to lactic acid bacteria, the family of microorganisms responsible for this type of fermentation. Lactic acid bacteria are naturally present on raw vegetables. When you create the right conditions — anaerobic (no oxygen) and with enough salt — those bacteria multiply and ferment the vegetable carbohydrates into lactic acid.

That lactic acid is what preserves the food. It drops the pH and creates an acidic environment that's hostile to harmful bacteria. It also creates the sour, tangy flavor that makes fermented vegetables distinctive. And as long as you don't heat the finished product, the live bacteria remain active — that's what makes these foods probiotic.

Research published in the NCBI Bookshelf on lactic acid fermentations describes the process plainly: the raw vegetable is shredded, salt is added at approximately 2 percent by weight, the salt draws out moisture to create a brine, and anaerobic conditions are maintained. The rest is biology.

## The Names From Around the World

Every major food culture developed its own version of lacto-fermented vegetables. Same process, different ingredients, different flavors, different names.

**Sauerkraut** — German for "sour cabbage" — is the version most North Americans know first. Shredded cabbage, salt, time. Simple. Tart. Crispy when it's young, softer and more complex as it ages. Most people have at least encountered it, even if they've only had the pasteurized supermarket version.

**Kimchi** is the Korean tradition, and it's a little more elaborate — napa cabbage or daikon radish, gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes), garlic, ginger, fish sauce or salted shrimp, sometimes other vegetables. Hundreds of varieties exist. The bacterial communities in kimchi are shaped by those additional ingredients, but the underlying mechanism is the same.

**Curtido** is a Central American fermented cabbage relish — lighter fermentation, typically with oregano and lime, eaten alongside pupusas. **Tsukemono** is the Japanese tradition of pickled vegetables, some of which are lacto-fermented, others salt-pressed or marinated in vinegar. **Kvass** in Eastern Europe is traditionally made by fermenting bread or vegetables — beet kvass is a classic.

Research published in PMC on the microbial communities of 75 homemade fermented vegetables found remarkable diversity across traditions — different vegetables, different bacterial strains, different flavor profiles — but a consistent underlying process of lactic acid fermentation driving them all.

## Fermented Vegetables vs. Pickles: The Crucial Distinction

This is the part that trips people up, and it matters.

Pickles you find in most grocery stores are not fermented. They're cucumbers submerged in a vinegar brine. The vinegar creates an acidic environment that prevents spoilage, and the cucumbers absorb that sour flavor. There are no live bacteria in commercial vinegar pickles.

Traditional dill pickles — made the old way, in a salt brine, left at room temperature for a few weeks — are fermented. They're lacto-fermented cucumbers. The bacteria naturally on the cucumber's skin go to work in that salt brine, producing lactic acid and generating billions of live bacteria.

The rule is simple: if it's made with vinegar, it's not a probiotic food. If it's made with salt and time, it is. Read the label. A genuine lacto-fermented pickle will say "raw," "naturally fermented," or "no vinegar added." It will be refrigerated. The brine will be slightly cloudy.

UC Davis's fermentation research program has documented this distinction thoroughly. Lacto-fermented vegetables and vinegar pickles are fundamentally different products despite looking and tasting similar.

## Why Raw Vegetables Are Already Prepared for Fermentation

Here's something that genuinely blew my mind when I first thought about it. The bacteria responsible for lacto-fermentation — the Lactobacillus, Leuconostoc, and Pediococcus species that do the work — are already on the surface of your vegetables. Every unwashed, fresh cabbage leaf. Every cucumber straight from the garden. Every carrot, radish, turnip.

These bacteria are part of the vegetable's own life web. They're living on the plant the same way soil bacteria live in the ground — part of a complex ecosystem the plant participates in. When you wash your vegetables, you wash some off. When you cook them, you kill them all. When you ferment them, you invite them to transform the food in a way that benefits you.

This is basically what Albert Howard meant when he wrote about the web of life connecting plants to soil to people. The food itself carries life — and traditional fermentation, developed over thousands of years, honored and extended that life.

## The Diversity of What You Can Ferment

Almost any vegetable can be lacto-fermented. Cabbage is the classic because its cell structure and natural bacterial population are well-suited to the process. But the tradition is far broader.

Successful ferments include cucumbers, carrots, beets, turnips, cauliflower, green beans, garlic, onions, peppers, and dozens of others. Mix-vegetable ferments are common — kimchi often contains radish alongside the cabbage; mixed pickle crocks have been a fixture of household food preservation across cultures for centuries.

The salt percentage, temperature, and duration all affect the outcome. A 2% brine at room temperature produces a mild product in five to seven days. A 3% brine fermented cool and slow over several weeks produces something more complex, more sour, more deeply flavored — and potentially with a more diverse bacterial community.

Experimenting is part of the tradition. Different vegetables, different salt levels, different herbs and spices — every variation produces something new. That variety also means your gut is being exposed to a wider range of bacterial strains and bioactive compounds.

Y'all, these foods aren't a trend. They're a tradition. The terminology is new but the biology is ancient, and your body was built to recognize it.

---

scottyQuotes: The bacteria responsible for fermentation are already on your vegetables. Every fresh cabbage leaf, every cucumber from the garden. You're not adding life — you're inviting the life that's already there to do its work.
If it's made with vinegar, it's not a probiotic food. If it's made with salt and time, it is. Read the label.
These foods aren't a trend. They're a tradition. The terminology is new but the biology is ancient, and your body was built to recognize it.
